items.creator: Data Friendly Space
items.title: Myanmar Crisis Situation Analysis (Period: 29/06/26 - 05/07/26)
items.author: Data Friendly Space
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: Data Friendly Space

Please refer to the attached file.

Myanmar has been in crisis since 1 February 2021, when the military (Tatmadaw) overthrew the democratically elected government.
This coup sparked widespread protests and armed resistance, leading to a severe political, economic, and humanitarian crisis. The
military regime, known as the State Administration Council (SAC), has responded with brutal force, resulting in thousands of civilian
deaths and widespread human rights abuses. As of March 2026, approximately 3.7 million people are internally displaced across the
country — a dramatic increase from roughly 300,000 pre-2021 — with overall displacement figures — including those who fled to
neighbouring countries — crossing 5.3 million.
In attempt to seek international legitimacy, the military held general elections in three phases over December 2025 and January 2026
for the first time since the coup. The overwhelming majority of seats were won by the pro-military Union Solidarity and Development
Party (USDP) and saw SAC chairman Min Aung Hlaing elected as president in April 2026. The elections have been widely denounced as
neither free nor fair. Despite the transition to a quasi-civilian presidency, armed clashes have continued in multiple parts of the
country, including Sagaing, Magway, northern Shan, Kayah, Kayin, Kachin, Chin, and Rakhine. The Arakan Army (AA) now controls all
but three of Rakhine State's 17 townships, while resistance forces including the Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA) have
consolidated influence across parts of central and southeastern Myanmar. The UN's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan
(HNRP), released 10 December 2025, projects over 16 million people will require humanitarian assistance this year.
Recovery from the March 2025 earthquake remains slow, with an estimated 3.5 million tonnes of debris and reconstruction hampered
by insecurity, shortages of heavy machinery and labour, and limited access. Many affected communities, particularly in Magway,
Sagaing, and Mandalay, continue to live in temporary shelters, while essential services remain disrupted. Approximately 24,200 houses
were damaged (24% completely destroyed), and 132 health facilities were damaged — half in Mandalay.
The Rohingya crisis continues to worsen, with the junta's blockade of Rakhine preventing international aid delivery since late 2023,
leaving hundreds of thousands of IDPs with severely limited assistance. On 10 December 2025, a military airstrike destroyed Mrauk-U
General Hospital, killing at least 33 people and injuring 76-80 — one of the deadliest attacks on healthcare in the conflict. Chronic
poverty now affects over 80% of the population; the World Bank projects GDP will contract 2.0% in FY2025/26 with inflation remaining
above 20%, and market disruptions have deepened food insecurity nationwide. The 2026 HNRP appeals for approximately USD 890
million but reflects severely constrained funding and narrowed operational space, forcing agencies to prioritise only 2.6 million people
with the most severe needs for life-saving support.

items.creator: Data Friendly Space
items.title: Myanmar Crisis Situation Analysis (Period: 15/06/26 - 21/06/26)
items.author: Data Friendly Space
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: Data Friendly Space

Please refer to the attached file.

Myanmar has been in crisis since 1 February 2021, when the military (Tatmadaw) overthrew the democratically elected government.

This coup sparked widespread protests and armed resistance, leading to a severe political, economic, and humanitarian crisis. The military regime, known as the State Administration Council (SAC), has responded with brutal force, resulting in thousands of civilian deaths and widespread human rights abuses. As of March 2026, approximately 3.7 million people are internally displaced across the country — a dramatic increase from roughly 300,000 pre-2021 — with overall displacement figures — including those who fled to neighbouring countries — crossing 5.3 million.

In attempt to seek international legitimacy, the military held general elections in three phases over December 2025 and January 2026 for the first time since the coup. The overwhelming majority of seats were won by the pro-military Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) and saw SAC chairman Min Aung Hlaing elected as president in April 2026. The elections have been widely denounced as neither free nor fair. Despite the transition to a quasi-civilian presidency, armed clashes have continued in multiple parts of the country, including Sagaing, Magway, northern Shan, Kayah, Kayin, Kachin, Chin, and Rakhine. The Arakan Army (AA) now controls all but three of Rakhine State's 17 townships, while resistance forces including the Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA) have consolidated influence across parts of central and southeastern Myanmar. The UN's 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan (HNRP), released 10 December 2025, projects over 16 million people will require humanitarian assistance this year.
Recovery from the March 2025 earthquake remains slow, with an estimated 3.5 million tonnes of debris and reconstruction hampered by insecurity, shortages of heavy machinery and labour, and limited access. Many affected communities, particularly in Magway,
Sagaing, and Mandalay, continue to live in temporary shelters, while essential services remain disrupted. Approximately 24,200 houses were damaged (24% completely destroyed), and 132 health facilities were damaged — half in Mandalay.

The Rohingya crisis continues to worsen, with the junta's blockade of Rakhine preventing international aid delivery since late 2023, leaving hundreds of thousands of IDPs with severely limited assistance. On 10 December 2025, a military airstrike destroyed Mrauk-U General Hospital, killing at least 33 people and injuring 76-80 — one of the deadliest attacks on healthcare in the conflict. Chronic poverty now affects over 80% of the population; the World Bank projects GDP will contract 2.0% in FY2025/26 with inflation remaining above 20%, and market disruptions have deepened food insecurity nationwide. The 2026 HNRP appeals for approximately USD 890 million but reflects severely constrained funding and narrowed operational space, forcing agencies to prioritise only 2.6 million people with the most severe needs for life-saving support.

items.creator: International Rescue Committee
items.title: Somalia: El Niño threatens deadly floods and disease across East Africa and Asia
items.author: International Rescue Committee
items.content:
Countries: Somalia, Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Kenya, Pakistan, Uganda
Source: International Rescue Committee

New York, NY, July 13, 2026 — An intensifying El Niño weather pattern threatens to bring severe flooding, disease outbreaks, heatwaves and drought to communities across East Africa and Asia in the coming weeks, the International Rescue Committee warned today, with families in Kenya, Uganda, Somalia, Bangladesh, Pakistan and Afghanistan among the most impacted.

In Somalia, June data pointed to a 60% chance of above-average rainfall across the south and southwest, and forecasters are watching a July 15 update that will determine funding and planning for anticipatory action. Overlapping crises of drought and displacement have left 4.8 million people in Somalia in need of urgent aid, a number expected to climb as El Niño flooding compounds the drought in coming months. A flood in 2023 destroyed almost 13,000 tons of crops and damaged entire towns and cities, and experts warn that a similar storm would do more damage this time, as communities already worn down by drought and reduced humanitarian funding have fewer resources and coping mechanisms to rely upon.

Heavy rain in the Ethiopian highlands combined with local Deyr season rains could send river levels rising quickly along Somalia's two main waterways, contaminating water sources and raising the risk of cholera and acute watery diarrhea. The anticipated impacts are regional as Kenya faces an 80–82% chance of El Niño persisting through 2026, with dry conditions this summer giving way to a high risk of flooding and landslides later in the year, prompting the government to activate its national response framework. Uganda anticipates a similar shift from drier months to a flood-prone final quarter, raising fears of displacement and disease after more than 413,000 people were affected in the last El Niño cycle.

"We're watching several emergencies converge at once, and the places least equipped to absorb another shock are the ones in the crosshairs," said Bob Kitchen, IRC Vice President for Emergencies. "Acting now, before the rain falls, is far cheaper and far more humane than responding after people have lost everything."

The same El Niño pattern is expected to hit Asia on a different front, pushing seasonal rainfall below normal and temperatures higher across Pakistan even as northern mountain areas face sudden glacier-melt floods. Meanwhile, Bangladesh's monsoon season has already turned deadly this year, and landslides and flooding have killed at least 15 Rohingya refugees living in the Cox's Bazar camps and displaced more than 10,000 people since the start of July. In Afghanistan, El Nino conditions are expected to result in above average rainfall, putting large swathes of the country at risk of flooding. In response to increased climate risk, the IRC's anticipatory action model already delivers cash to at-risk families ahead of disaster, helping them buy food, pay for water and protect livestock rather than face impossible choices like pulling children from school or arranging early marriages for their daughters.

In the face of a strengthening El Nino, the IRC is calling on donors and governments to fund more anticipatory action activities across East Africa and Asia now, rather than waiting for disaster to strike. Early funding would allow the IRC and partners to reach families in impacted areas with cash, clean water and early warnings before the worst hits, thereby saving lives, preserving resources, and reducing suffering.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: Somalia, Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Kenya, Pakistan, Uganda
Source: International Rescue Committee
New York, NY, July 13, 2026 — An intensifying El Niño weather pattern threatens to bring severe flooding, disease outbreaks, heatwaves and drought to communities across East Africa and Asia in the coming weeks, the International Rescue Committee warned today, with families in Kenya, Uganda, Somalia, Bangladesh, Pakistan and Afghanistan among the most impacted.

The anticipated impacts are regional as Kenya faces an 80–82% chance of El Niño persisting through 2026, with dry conditions this summer giving way to a high risk of flooding and landslides later in the year, prompting the government to activate its national response framework. Uganda anticipates a similar shift from drier months to a flood-prone final quarter, raising fears of displacement and disease after more than 413,000 people were affected in the last El Niño cycle.
"We're watching several emergencies converge at once, and the places least equipped to absorb another shock are the ones in the crosshairs," said Bob Kitchen, IRC Vice President for Emergencies. "Acting now, before the rain falls, is far cheaper and far more humane than responding after people have lost everything."
The same El Niño pattern is expected to hit Asia on a different front, pushing seasonal rainfall below normal and temperatures higher across Pakistan even as northern mountain areas face sudden glacier-melt floods. In Afghanistan, El Nino conditions are expected to result in above average rainfall, putting large swathes of the country at risk of flooding. Early funding would allow the IRC and partners to reach families in impacted areas with cash, clean water and early warnings before the worst hits, thereby saving lives, preserving resources, and reducing suffering.

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: Pakistan: Violence Against Health Care in Conflict 2025
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Countries: Pakistan, Afghanistan
Sources: Insecurity Insight, Safeguarding Health in Conflict

Please refer to the attached files.

Key findings:

  • Incidents continued to be recorded in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa’s southern districts of Bannu, Dera Ismail Khan, and Malakand, which are known for security challenges and militant activity.
  • In 2025, the pattern of violence against health care in Pakistan shifted: incidents damaging health care facilities increased sharply, while attacks on polio vaccination campaigns and their security escorts and health worker killings continued, but at lower levels than in previous years, representing a smaller proportion of overall incidents. The overall number of incidents in which health workers were kidnapped remained similar to that in 2024; however, their proportion of incidents and the number of victims increased.
  • Most reported incidents were attributed to unidentified armed men, while TTP militants were named in nine cases in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. Unlike in previous years, when incidents attributed to the group mainly targeted polio vaccination campaigns, 2025 saw an increase in TTP militants detonating improvised explosive devices (IEDs) that damaged health facilities.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: Pakistan, Afghanistan
Sources: Insecurity Insight, Safeguarding Health in Conflict

Please refer to the attached files.
Key findings:
Incidents continued to be recorded in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa’s southern districts of Bannu, Dera Ismail Khan, and Malakand, which are known for security challenges and militant activity.
In 2025, the pattern of violence against health care in Pakistan shifted: incidents damaging health care facilities increased sharply, while attacks on polio vaccination campaigns and their security escorts and health worker killings continued, but at lower levels than in previous years, representing a smaller proportion of overall incidents. The overall number of incidents in which health workers were kidnapped remained similar to that in 2024; however, their proportion of incidents and the number of victims increased.
Most reported incidents were attributed to unidentified armed men, while TTP militants were named in nine cases in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. Unlike in previous years, when incidents attributed to the group mainly targeted polio vaccination campaigns, 2025 saw an increase in TTP militants detonating improvised explosive devices (IEDs) that damaged health facilities.

items.creator: UNOPS
items.title: Syria: Funded by KSrelief, UNOPS started equipping three hospitals in Hama in coordination with the Ministry of Health
items.author: UNOPS
items.content:
Country: Syrian Arab Republic
Source: UNOPS

Please refer to the attached files.

Funded by KSrelief, UNOPS started equipping three hospitals in Hama in coordination with the Ministry of Health

Hama, July 7, 2026 – With funding from the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia through King Salman humanitarian aid and relief Centre (KSrelief), UNOPS, in close coordination with the Syrian Ministry of Health, has started equipping three hospitals and establishing paediatric intensive care units (ICUs) in Hama governorate - namely Hama Maternity and Children's Hospital, As-Salamiyeh National Hospital, and As-Suqaylabiyah National Hospital.

These newly established ICUs will provide critical specialized care, addressing a significant gap in pediatric healthcare within the Hama governorate. By strengthening the localized operational capacity of these hospitals, the initiative aims to improve survival rates and the management of life-threatening conditions in children.

Furthermore, UNOPS will provide comprehensive training for healthcare providers and technical staff in Hama to ensure the effective use and maintenance of the new equipment. This capacity-building component is designed to guarantee long-term functionality and enhance the overall quality and sustainability of pediatric health service delivery.

This initiative is part of the “Supporting Children's hospitals in northern Syria with specialized medical equipment for intensive care, cardiac care and surgeries” project. The project aims to contribute to the UN Strategic Framework (UNSF) 2022-2026 Syria and SDG3: good health and wellbeing.

  • END -

About KSrelief:

The King Salman Humanitarian Aid and Relief Centre (KSrelief), established under the guidance of the Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques, King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud, represents Saudi Arabia's commitment to global humanitarian efforts. Inaugurated in May 2015, KSrelief has provided impartial humanitarian aid and relief to over 100 countries in cooperation with local, regional, and international organizations.

Stay updated: Twitter: @KSrelief_EN | Instagram: @KSrelief | LinkedIn: KSrelief | Website: www.ksrelief.org

About UNOPS:

UNOPS offers practical solutions to the UN and other partners, across peace and security, humanitarian and development operations. UNOPS mission is to help people build better lives and countries achieve peace and sustainable development. We help the United Nations, governments and other partners to manage projects, and deliver sustainable infrastructure and procurement in an efficient way. Read more: www.unops.org

UNOPS Amman Multi-Country Office implements projects in Jordan, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria, and Yemen and implements work in partnership with bilateral and multilateral donors, national governments and other UN agencies.

For Press Inquiries, please contact:

Anwar Abu Sakieneh, Communications Specialist (UNOPS)

Anwarab@unops.org I +962 7 9902 6315 I +962 6 5902122

items.creator: UNOPS
items.title: Syria: Funded by KSrelief, UNOPS started establishing the Pediatric Cardiac Surgery Department at Ibn Roshed Hospital in coordination with the Ministry of Health
items.author: UNOPS
items.content:
Country: Syrian Arab Republic
Source: UNOPS

Please refer to the attached files.

Funded by KSrelief, UNOPS started establishing the Pediatric Cardiac Surgery Department at Ibn Roshed Hospital in coordination with the Ministry of Health

Aleppo, July 7, 2026 – With funding from the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia through King Salman humanitarian aid and relief Centre (KSrelief) and in close coordination with the Syrian Ministry of Health, UNOPS has started equipping the Ibn Roshed Hospital for Cardiac Diseases and Surgery to establish a Department of Pediatric Cardiac Surgery.

This newly established department will provide critical care for children with heart conditions, enhancing the hospital’s operational capacity to diagnose, treat, and manage pediatric cardiac diseases, and ultimately improving children’s health outcomes.

In addition, UNOPS will deliver comprehensive training in Aleppo for healthcare providers, technical staff and other relevant personnel on the effective use and maintenance of the equipment. This capacity-building effort aims to ensure long-term functionality while enhancing the quality and sustainability of pediatric health service delivery.

This initiative is part of the “Supporting Children's hospitals in northern Syria with specialized medical equipment for intensive care, cardiac care and surgeries” project. The project aims to contribute to the UN Strategic Framework (UNSF) 2022-2026 Syria and SDG3: good health and wellbeing.

  • END -

About KSrelief:

The King Salman Humanitarian Aid and Relief Centre (KSrelief), established under the guidance of the Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques, King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud, represents Saudi Arabia's commitment to global humanitarian efforts. Inaugurated in May 2015, KSrelief has provided impartial humanitarian aid and relief to over 100 countries in cooperation with local, regional, and international organizations.

Stay updated: Twitter: @KSrelief_EN | Instagram: @KSrelief | LinkedIn: KSrelief | Website: www.ksrelief.org

About UNOPS:

UNOPS offers practical solutions to the UN and other partners, across peace and security, humanitarian and development operations. UNOPS mission is to help people build better lives and countries achieve peace and sustainable development. We help the United Nations, governments and other partners to manage projects, and deliver sustainable infrastructure and procurement in an efficient way. Read more: www.unops.org

UNOPS Amman Multi-Country Office implements projects in Jordan, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria, and Yemen and implements work in partnership with bilateral and multilateral donors, national governments and other UN agencies.

For Press Inquiries, please contact:

Anwar Abu Sakieneh, Communications Specialist (UNOPS)

Anwarab@unops.org I +962 7 9902 6315 I +962 6 5902122
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Syrian Arab Republic
Source: UNOPS

Please refer to the attached files.
Funded by KSrelief, UNOPS started establishing the Pediatric Cardiac Surgery Department at Ibn Roshed Hospital in coordination with the Ministry of Health
Aleppo, July 7, 2026 – With funding from the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia through King Salman humanitarian aid and relief Centre (KSrelief) and in close coordination with the Syrian Ministry of Health, UNOPS has started equipping the Ibn Roshed Hospital for Cardiac Diseases and Surgery to establish a Department of Pediatric Cardiac Surgery.
This newly established department will provide critical care for children with heart conditions, enhancing the hospital’s operational capacity to diagnose, treat, and manage pediatric cardiac diseases, and ultimately improving children’s health outcomes.
In addition, UNOPS will deliver comprehensive training in Aleppo for healthcare providers, technical staff and other relevant personnel on the effective use and maintenance of the equipment. This capacity-building effort aims to ensure long-term functionality while enhancing the quality and sustainability of pediatric health service delivery.
This initiative is part of the “Supporting Children's hospitals in northern Syria with specialized medical equipment for intensive care, cardiac care and surgeries” project. The project aims to contribute to the UN Strategic Framework (UNSF) 2022-2026 Syria and SDG3: good health and wellbeing.
END -

items.creator: Médecins Sans Frontières
items.title: Ukraine: No Safe Place to Heal
items.author: Médecins Sans Frontières
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Source: Médecins Sans Frontières

Please refer to the attached file.

Médecins Sans Frontières’ (MSF’s) latest report, No Safe Place to Heal, documents relentless attacks on healthcare and medical personnel in Ukraine, which appear to constitute a deliberate strategy to destroy the medical system and collectively punish people — rather than being an incidental product of Russia's invasion.

Country: Ukraine
Source: Médecins Sans Frontières

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Ukraine: Humanitarian Access Severity Overview (May 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

In May 2026, OCHA conducted a humanitarian access severity monitoring exercise to assess humanitarian actors’ perceptions of access constraints and their ability to conduct operations across Ukraine. Given the different access dynamics across the country, the exercise used two levels of analysis: hromada-level analysis in conflict-affected
front-line and border oblasts, and raion-level analysis in central and western Ukraine.

In the 521 conflict-affected hromadas assessed in front-line and border oblasts, 251 hromadas were classified as having high or extremely high access difficulties, up from 242 hromadas in October 2025. The deterioration was most significant at the most severe end of the scale, with hromadas classified as having extremely high access constraints or no access (level 4) increasing from 196 to 211. At the same time, the number of hromadas classified as accessible or having low access constraints (level 1) declined from 243 to 225.

Access deterioration was driven primarily by the proliferation of drones, recurrent attacks affecting humanitarian personnel, assets and facilities, and increasingly constrained operating conditions in border areas. These factors reduced operational predictability and limited humanitarian presence, including in areas where the front line did not shift substantially. Overall, partners reported deterioration in access conditions in 42 hromadas. Of these, 15 hromadas worsened from high access difficulties (level 3) to extremely high access constraints or no access (level 4), mostly in Zaporizka and Khersonska oblasts, where five and four hromadas respectively moved into the most
severe category.

The highest concentrations of hromadas with high access difficulties were recorded in areas directly affected by the armed conflict, including areas along the front line or exposed to active hostilities, cross-border attacks, drone activity, mines and unexploded ordnance, and movement restrictions. In these areas, humanitarian operations may still take place, but are often intermittent, highly constrained or dependent on specific windows of access.

Most hromadas assessed as having extremely high access constraints or no access (level 4) were in territories of Ukraine occupied by the Russian Federation. During the exercise, these areas remained inaccessible to humanitarian actors operating from areas under the control of the Government of Ukraine. The access situation for these hromadas was assessed in the most severe category (level 4), with no breakdown by specific type of access impediment.

In central and western Ukraine, 74 raions were assessed. Of these, 71 raions were classified as accessible or having low access constraints (level 1), while three raions, all in Odeska Oblast, were classified as having moderate access constraints (level 2).

items.creator: UNOPS
items.title: International and national partners mark the successful completion of the "Labor-Intensive Maintenance in Public Schools" project in Jordan
items.author: UNOPS
items.content:
Country: Jordan
Source: UNOPS

Please refer to the attached files.

International and national partners mark the successful completion of the "Labor-Intensive Maintenance in Public Schools" project in Jordan

Amman, July 12, 2026 – Today, representatives from the Ministry of Education, the Ministry of Labour, the German Embassy in Jordan, the German Federal Ministry of Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ), KfW Development Bank and UNOPS marked the successful completion of the "Labor-Intensive Maintenance in Public Schools in Jordan" project.

Implemented over two phases during the past seven years through the strategic partnership between the German government, Jordanian government and UNOPS, the project responded to the dual challenges of strained service delivery and economic hardship. The project supported livelihoods through creating short term employment opportunities for both vulnerable Jordanians and Syrian refugees, building the workers’ practical skills to enhance their future employability while supporting public schools across Jordan.

Dr. Sahar Shakhtreh, the Secretary-General for Administrative and Financial Affairs at the Ministry of Education, on behalf of the Minister of Education in Jordan Dr. Azmi Mahafzah, noted: “This project aligns with our national priorities, as it contributed to the implementation of Jordan’s response plan to the Syrian crisis by providing employment opportunities and supporting host communities. It also reinforced the strategic plan for the education sector by improving the school environment and maintaining public schools, which is also consistent with the preventive maintenance program implemented by the ministry. The project is also linked to the ‘Partnership for the Future’ initiative of the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development.”

The project’s cash for work modality has improved livelihoods through generating over 16,000 temporary employment opportunities in cleaning and maintaining over 660 public schools, contributing to an improved and healthier learning environment that enhanced the educational experience for more than 250,000 students, 42 percent of whom are girls.

Mr. Ibrahim Al-Saket, Assistant Secretary-General, Ministry of Labour, said: “The Ministry of Labour has been closely involved with this project since its inception. We are pleased that 37 percent of the participants were women, which aligns with our national policy to empower women in the workforce. The skills acquired by both Jordanian and Syrian refugee workers will play a pivotal role in improving their livelihoods and long-term career prospects.”

Reflecting the project’s commitment to inclusion and equal access to work opportunities, more than one-third of participants were women and 6% were persons with disabilities. In addition, by balancing participation between Jordanians and Syrian refugees, the project reinforced the social fabric and stability of host communities across Jordan.

Dr. Mattias Schmidt-Rosen, Director, KfW office in Amman, stated: “Germany and KfW Development Bank are proud to support Jordan’s efforts to improve public services while expanding economic opportunities for vulnerable Jordanians and Syrian refugees. This project demonstrates that investing in public infrastructure can also strengthen livelihoods, develop skills, and generate lasting social and economic benefits. We are pleased to have supported this successful initiative and look forward to continuing our partnership with Jordan in advancing sustainable and inclusive development.”

Muhammad Usman Akram, Representative and Director of UNOPS Multi-Country Office in Amman, said: “I extend my profound appreciation to the government of Germany and the KfW Development Bank for their sustained trust and confidence in UNOPS. I also thank our national counterparts for their continued support and guidance to ensure our operations remained synchronized with Jordan’s development path.” He continued, “by aligning our efforts with Jordan’s national vision, we have transformed infrastructure maintenance into tangible, high-quality results for local communities. This collaboration demonstrates that practical, cash for work interventions can contribute to stability while addressing national priorities.”

  • END -

About UNOPS:

UNOPS offers practical solutions to the UN and other partners, across peace and security, humanitarian and development operations. UNOPS mission is to help people build better lives and countries achieve peace and sustainable development. We help the United Nations, governments and other partners to manage projects, and deliver sustainable infrastructure and procurement in an efficient way. Read more: www.unops.org

UNOPS Multi-Country Office in Amman implements projects in Jordan, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria, and Yemen and implements work in partnership with bilateral and multilateral donors, national governments and other UN agencies.

For Press Inquiries, please contact:

Anwar Abu Sakieneh, Communications Specialist (UNOPS)

AnwarAB@unops.org I +962 7 9902 6315 or +962 6 5902122

items.title: Ukraine - Quarterly Operation Overview, April - June, 2026
items.author: Logistics Cluster
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Sources: Logistics Cluster, World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

Highlights

  • 94 partners supported through logistics coordination and information sharing, access to common services and trainings

• 20 participants from 9 organizations to the Logistics Cluster Induction training in Kyiv • IMPACCT Customs & Importations course for the first time launched in Ukrainian

  • 352 service requests from partners processed

  • 11 inter-agency convoys supported, delivering 60 MT of relief items to 12 hard-to-reach locations

items.creator: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.title: Lebanon Response Plan: Lebanon Logistics and Telecoms Cluster At a Glance Q1 2026
items.author: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

The Logistics and Telecommunications Cluster (LTC) Q1 Dashboard presents a snapshot of the cluster’s activities and achievements. Between 2 and 16 March 2026, the team conducted extensive gaps and needs analysis and deep assessments to evaluate the impact of the escalation in Lebanon. The findings from these assessments informed operational planning and triggered the activation of the Cluster on the 16 March. The Cluster’s Logistics response focuses on coordination, information management, and facilitating access to common services, including storage and transport through convoys to hard-to-reach areas. The Telecommunications response focuses on coordination with government counterparts and humanitarian partners, as well as the deployment of emergency telecommunications equipment and services across the country where it is needed the most.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: WFP Dominican Republic Country Brief July 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Dominican Republic
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

• The Dominican Republic, like other Small Island Developing States in the Caribbean, is highly exposed to severe weather events such as hurricanes, flooding, and droughts, which disrupt livelihoods, damage infrastructure, and strain food systems. Ecosystem degradation further reduces natural protection against extreme events. Around 25 percent of households are highly vulnerable to climate shocks, disproportionately affecting poor and hazard‑prone communities.
Territorial inequalities leave rural and border provinces with fewer opportunities and higher poverty rates, while 53.4 percent of employment is informal. Global food price volatility, dependence on remittances, and localized migration pressures compound these risks, intersecting with persistent micronutrient deficiencies and the double burden of malnutrition disproportionately affecting women, children, and excluded groups. These vulnerabilities are exacerbated by global market instability linked to the Middle East crisis, driving food price volatility, energy cost increases, and supply chain disruption, raising concern for national food security and household resilience.

• WFP’s strategy links national planning with local action to address recurrent shocks systemically. Evidence and data guide interventions, building government capacity so central decisions translate into community protection. Climate services and risk management tools are embedded in social protection, enabling anticipatory action. Pre‑positioning, disaster risk finance ensure rapid response when systems are strained.
Platforms such as ANACAONA and the DR–Haiti corridor exemplify collective resilience.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: WFP Zimbabwe Country Brief, June 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Zimbabwe
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

• Zimbabwe is closely monitoring forecasts from the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), which indicate the potential for El Niño conditions during the 2026/2027 agricultural season. While more localised and detailed forecasts from the National Climate Outlook Forum are expected in August, the Government of Zimbabwe, supported by WFP and partners, is enhancing preparedness through anticipatory action, climate risk monitoring, early warning systems and contingency planning to safeguard food security, protect livelihoods and reduce the impact of potential climate-related shocks on vulnerable communities.

• Zimbabwe continues to face intersecting climatic and structural pressures that are driving food insecurity and livelihood stress. Recurrent climate shocks have disrupted agricultural production, increasing vulnerability in both rural and urban areas.

• Through its 2022–2026 Country Strategic Plan,
WFP Zimbabwe is delivering life‑saving food and nutrition assistance while scaling up resilience‑building efforts and strengthening early warning and social protection systems. At the same time, WFP is investing in government capacity to lead and ultimately assume full ownership of programme delivery, while partnering with the private sector to accelerate progress towards Zero Hunger.
